coredata.dc:title: Novel palladium nanoparticles/4-aminophenol functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots-based nanocomposite electrochemical sensor: Synthesis, characterization, capacitance, and dopamine sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Yağmur Kabaş, Sultan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, an ultrasensitive electrochemical sensor based on palladium nanoparticles/4-aminophenol functional nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs) composite was fabricated to detect dopamine (DA). The sensor was characterized by FTIR, UV–Vis, TEM, EDX, and XRD methods. The electrochemical analysis of the nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was conducted by cyclic voltammetry (CV). HOMO-LUMO energy levels and band gap energy was calculated from cyclic voltammograms, and the band gap energy was found to be small at 0.06 eV. The electrode reaction properties of dopamine on this developed ultrasensitive electrode were investigated by CV and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), which showed that the electrode had electrocatalytic activity for dopamine sensing. Moreover, the developed electrode exhibited good sensitivity and improved performance, and the linear calibration curve for dopamine was calculated to have a LOD (limit of detection) of 21 pM in the range of 250 pM to 10 nM. Finally, the selectivity of the GQDs nanocomposite modified GCE towards dopamine was studied in the presence of several bioactive compounds with inhibitory effects. In addition, the specific capacitance of the nanocomposite material at 5 mV s−1 was found to be as high as 0.777F g−1. Furthermore, the dopamine selectivity of the sensor among some organic and inorganic interfering bioanalytes was investigated and the sensor was found to have excellent DA selectivity despite the bioanalytes.

head.ce:title: Novel palladium nanoparticles/4-aminophenol functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots-based nanocomposite electrochemical sensor: Synthesis, characterization, capacitance, and dopamine sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Processing Technologies Programs, Lapseki Vocational School, Çanakkale Onsekiz Mart University, TR-17800 Çanakkale, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Processing Technologies Programs

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Vocational School of Department of Chemistry and Chemical Processing Technologies, Çanakkale Onsekiz Mart University, TR-17100 Labseki-Çanakkale, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, an ultrasensitive electrochemical sensor based on palladium nanoparticles/4-aminophenol functional nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs) composite was fabricated to detect dopamine (DA). The sensor was characterized by FTIR, UV–Vis, TEM, EDX, and XRD methods. The electrochemical analysis of the nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was conducted by cyclic voltammetry (CV). HOMO-LUMO energy levels and band gap energy was calculated from cyclic voltammograms, and the band gap energy was found to be small at 0.06 eV. The electrode reaction properties of dopamine on this developed ultrasensitive electrode were investigated by CV and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), which showed that the electrode had electrocatalytic activity for dopamine sensing. Moreover, the developed electrode exhibited good sensitivity and improved performance, and the linear calibration curve for dopamine was calculated to have a LOD (limit of detection) of 21 pM in the range of 250 pM to 10 nM. Finally, the selectivity of the GQDs nanocomposite modified GCE towards dopamine was studied in the presence of several bioactive compounds with inhibitory effects. In addition, the specific capacitance of the nanocomposite material at 5 mV s−1 was found to be as high as 0.777F g−1. Furthermore, the dopamine selectivity of the sensor among some organic and inorganic interfering bioanalytes was investigated and the sensor was found to have excellent DA selectivity despite the bioanalytes.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is an important neurotransmitter involved in nerve signaling and is associated with some diseases. Abnormal levels of neurotransmitters have been linked with physical, psychotic and neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, dementia, addiction, depression, and schizophrenia [1–3]. It is therefore important to detect DA in patients to regulate body function. Various techniques for the selective and sensitive detection of DA include fluorimetry [4,5], chemiluminescence [6,7], capillary electrophoresis [8,9], and liquid chromatography [10,11]. Alternatively, the recently emerged electrochemical method has attracted considerable interest as a fast, reliable, simple, and low-cost process due to the direct interaction between the analyte and the electrode surface [12,13,22–29,14–21]. Over the past decade, novel biosensors have been developed using either carbon-based, metal-based, noble metals, or polymers nanomaterials that interact with target species that produce enzymes, DNA, or electrochemical responses that are detectable in various biological and environmental samples [30–32]. The advantages of electrochemical sensors include fast response, high sensitivity, specificity, and selectivity in combination with the electrocatalytic activity of target molecules [33].

An electrochemical biosensor for the detection of DA based on the incorporation of acid-functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) have been developed [14]. The reported sensor (GQDs-MWCNTs/GCE) was found to exhibit good electrocatalytic activity against the oxidation of DA and spiked DA in the dynamic linear range of 0.25–250 μM with low detection limits of 95 nM and 110 nM (S/N = 3), respectively [22]. Cubic palladium (Pd) and reduced graphene oxide modified GCE (Pd/RGO/GCE) was fabricated to detect DA by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) methods. Compared with Pd/GCE and RGO/GCE, Pd/RGO/GCE was found to exhibit excellent electrochemical activity. Using DPV, the calibration curves for DA were found to be linear with a concentration range of 0.45–421 μM and the limit of detection (S/N = 3) was calculated as 0.18 μM and 1.6 μM, respectively [29].

To store electrical energy, high-performance batteries and supercapacitors are used in various electronic devices [34,35]. Supercapacitors are more useful than batteries due to their small size, light weight, high power density and long cycle life. They can be rapidly charged and discharged and have become the most current topic of technological research and development. For supercapacitor anodes, 3D nitrogen-doped GQDs and reduced graphene oxide composites (NGDQs/rGO2) were synthesized in a single step by hydrothermal methods [36]. It has been reported that synthesized NGDQs/rGO2 might be a potential candidate for high performance energy storage applications [36]. Studies have shown that nitrogen and oxygen co-doped GQDs (NO-GQDs) used as electrode materials can improve the performance of micro-supercapacitors [37]. N-GQDs are promising for high performance supercapacitor applications. The simple hydrothermal production of these materials and their high capacitance properties makes them excellent for the development of supercapacitor electrodes with high energy density.

In addition, GQDs have attracted great interest due to their intrinsic qualities, such as large surface areas, unique optical properties, outstanding electrical performance, and have been applied in bioimaging, sensors, light emitting diodes, etc. [38–43]. One-pot synthesis of nitrogen-doped GQDs and their biomedical applications, such as bioimaging, detection of copper ions in living cells, cell viability, genotoxicity, and metal-nanocomposites have been studied [44–46]. Amine functionalized nitrogen-doped GQDs-based electrochemical sensors have been developed for the detection of dopamine [47,48] and heavy metal ions Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) [49]. Compared with other electrode-modified materials (including metal nanoparticles, polymers, etc.), graphene has outstanding conductivity, excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, is economical, and synthesis is simple [50–53]. Therefore, GQDs are also widely used in electrochemical sensors. To our knowledge, electrochemical sensors based on palladium nanoparticles/4-aminophenol functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (N-QDTs) composites have not yet been developed and their energy storage capacity has not been investigated.

In this study, 4-aminophenol-functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (4AP N-GQDs) were successfully prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid (CA) and 4AP as the carbon, nitrogen, and amino group precursors, respectively [45,46,54,55]. Palladium nanoparticle embedded/4AP N-GQD nanocomposites were prepared from the reaction of palladium nitrate and 4AP N-GQDs in aqueous media in a water bath at 90°C [46]. In the reaction, 4AP N-GQDs acted as a reducing and stabilizing agent to reduce palladium from Pd (II) to Pd (0) in aqueous media. The 4AP N-GQDs and PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites have been characterized by TEM, UV–Vis, FT-IR and EDX. The electrochemical analysis of PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite modified GCE was investigated by CV. HOMO-LUMO energy levels and band gap energy were calculated from cyclic voltammograms. The electrode reaction properties of DA on this ultrasensitive electrode were investigated by CV and DPV, and its selectivity towards DA was studied in the presence of various bioactive compounds with inhibitory effects. In addition, the specific capacitance of PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite was investigated.

All chemicals were obtained from commercial sources and used without further purification. Milli-Q water with a resistance greater than 18 MΩ was used in the experiments. Citric acid monohydrate (CA, ≥99.0%), 4-aminophenol (4AP, ≥98%), dopamine hydrochloride (DA, 99%), ascorbic acid (AA, 99%), , urea (ACS reagent, ≥99%), Zn(CH3COO)2 (≥98%), CaCl2 (anhydrous, powder, ≥97%), Na2SO4 (ACS reagent, ≥99.0%, anhydrous, powder), potassium hexacyanoferrate(II) trihydrate (K4Fe(CN)6·3H2O, ≥98.5%), potassium hexacyanoferrate(III) (K3Fe(CN)6, ≥99%), and potassium chloride (KCl, ≥99.0%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

4AP functionalized N-GQDs and PdNP/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites were synthesized according to existing protocols [45,46,54,55]. CA monohydrate (6.30 g, 30 mmol) and 4-AP (1.90 g, 10 mmol) were dissolved in 100 mL deionized distilled water and reacted in an autoclave at 200 °C for 18h. The resulting 4AP N-GQDs were stored in a vacuum desiccator for use (Scheme 1 ).

4AP N-GQDs (0.25 g) and Pd(NO3)2·H2O (0.25 g) were placed in a 250 mL round bottom flask and 100 mL of water was added. The mixture was heated in a water bath at 90 °C for 2 h to form PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites. After filtration, the powdered PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites were stored in a vacuum desiccator for further use (Scheme 1) [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of PdNPs/4AP N-doped GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Infrared absorption spectra were measured by a Perkin Elmer BX II spectrometer taken from KBr discs and were reported in cm−1 units. Absorption spectra (UV–Vis) were obtained using a PG Instruments T+80 UV/Vis spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analyses of 4AP N-GQDs and PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites were performed using a JEOL TEM-1400-EDX instrument.

The electrochemical measurements were taken by a CHI660D CH Instruments Electrochemical Workstation. The electrochemical properties of all the samples were studied by CV and DPV, with a three-electrode configuration containing a GCE (φ = 3 mm) as the working electrode, and the Ag/AgCl electrode and platinum wire as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. To prepare the modified GCE, the GCE was polished with Al2O3 powders and washed with both water and ethyl alcohol. After adding 10 μL of the stock solution of PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites prepared in water to the cleaned GCE surface, the GCE was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C and 0.2 bar pressure for 10 min and then used for analyses. After completion of one analysis, the GCE was cleaned and re-modified as aforementioned and used for a subsequent measurement (Scheme 2 ). For each measurement, the GCE surface was re-coated with PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs and measurements were taken after passing argon gas through the analyte solutions for 120 s. All electrochemical experiments were conducted in 0.1 M KCl electrolyte solution containing 50 µM K3Fe(CN)6 and K4Fe(CN)6 in the potential range of −0.2 to + 1.2 V by CV and DPV. A reproducible voltammogram was obtained under steady-state conditions. A Millipore Direct-Q® 3 UV Water Purification Systems by Merck was used for preparing all solutions. For the DPV analysis, the following parameters were used: pulse amplitude 50 mV, pulse time 0.04 s, voltage step 0.009 V, voltage step time 0.04, and potential step 10 mV. For CV analysis, the scan rates ranged from 5 mVs−1 to 200 mVs−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of synthesized PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites with FT-IR, UV–Vis, TEM, EDX and XRD
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectra of PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites exhibited characteristic changes in functional group frequencies when compared with the initial 4AP N-GQDs spectrum (Fig. 1 (a)). The fact that the FTIR spectra of the new material PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites are different from those of the starting material 4AP N-GQDs is evidence of its formation. In the FTIR spectrum of PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites, OH and Ar-H vibration bands were observed at 3422–3363 and 3197 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 1(a)). The COOH vibrations observed in a broad region in 4AP N-GQDs were not observed in PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites. This indicates that while Pd (II) ions are reduced to Pd (0) by 4AP N-GQDs, COOH groups in 4AP N-GQDs are oxidized to CO2 and replaced by OH groups. In PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites, CN, CC, CN and CO vibrations were observed at 1666, 1609, 1402 and 1402 cm−1, respectively. CN and CO overlapped in the spectrum. The absence of any peak after 1666 cm−1 in PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites indicates that COOH groups are oxidized during the reduction of Pd (II). FTIR results showed that both 4AP N-GQDs and its PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites were formed.

The absorption spectra of the suspensions of 4AP N-GQDs and PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites in water were measured (Fig. 1(b)). For the UV–Vis spectrum of 4AP N-GQDs, two bands assigned to the π-π* transitions of CC at 278 nm, and to the n-π* transitions of CN and CO at 360 nm were observed. As seen in Fig. 1 (b), for the UV–Vis spectrum of PdNPs/4AP N-GQD' nanocomposites, two bands assigned to π-π* transitions of CC at 244 nm and n-π* transitions of CN at 361 nm were observed. Also, the shoulder at 297 nm was assigned to plasmon resonance in PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites (Fig. 1(b)).

The structure and morphology of the 4AP N-GQDs and PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites were also confirmed by their TEM images as shown in Fig. 2 (a) and 2(b). TEM analysis confirmed the spherical shape of 4AP N-GQDs and PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites with particle sizes of approximately 8–11 and 10–13 nm.

The presence of elements in the structures of the materials was determined by EDX analyses, and the elements and their amounts are given in Fig. 2 (c) and 2(d). Carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and palladium were detected on the surface of the sample. The presence of palladium on the surface is evidence that the nanocomposite had formed (Fig. 2(d)). EDX only detects the content of elements on the surface and never provides a complete elemental analysis. However, EDX data confirmed the presence of nitrogen and palladium in the samples.

The XRD patterns of Pd-graphene nanocomposites were analyzed [56], and the typical peaks of graphene in the composite were observed at about 2θ= 26° and 2θ= 43°, and the characteristic peaks of Pd were observed around 2θ= 40.1°, 2θ= 46.6° and 2θ= 68.1°, respectively [56].

When the XRD pattern of PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites given in Fig. 2(e) were examined, the characteristic peaks of Pd were observed at 16.12°, 27.12°, 37.12°, 40.11°, and 52.11°. The characteristic peaks of 4AP N-GQDs in the nanocomposite were found at 11.11°, 44.12° and 61.11° (Fig. 2(e)).

The thickness of the layers of the PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite was calculated using the Bragg equation (2dSinθ = λ). The layer thickness was 0.381 nm for PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites. Where λ is the wavelength of the X-ray beam (λ= 0.154 nm), d is the distance between the layer distance between PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites adjacent sheets or layers, θ is the diffraction angle. As a result, the distance between the planes was calculated from the equation d=λ/2 sin(θ)=0.154 nm/2sin(θ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of PdNPs/4AP N-GDQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic performance of GCE, 4AP N-GQDs modified-GCE and PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite modified-GCE was studied in 0.1M KCl solution in the presence and absence of 50 µM K3Fe(CN)6 and K4Fe(CN)6 at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1 (Fig. 3 (a) and 3(b)). Fig. 3(b) shows that no electrode gave good results in the absence of K3Fe(CN)6 -3/-4, but in the presence of K3Fe(CN)6 -3/-4, only PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite modified-GCE produced an excellent result. Fig. 3(d) of when DA measurements were performed in the absence of K3Fe(CN)6 -3/-44 shows that the electrodes did not give good results. Based on these results, electrochemical biosensor studies were continued with PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites modified-GCE and in the presence of K3Fe(CN)6 -3/-4 [13–16]. The superior and selective sensing behavior of PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites towards DA can be attributed to the rich N-doped aromatic structure of 4AP N-GQD, which can promote closer and selective molecular interactions with DA, possibly by ring stacking, π-π or hydrogen bonding or a combination thereof, and to the increased conductivity and enhanced electron transfer ability due to in situ N doping (Scheme 3 ) [57]. In a study, it was observed that graphdiyne quantum dots (N-GDQDs) have stronger electrocatalytic oxidation ability than other electrodes, since the ΔEp potential difference is much smaller [48]. In the same study, the electrocatalytic behavior of the modified electrodes was investigated by EIS and it was found that (N-GDQDs) have lower resistance and the transfer of electrons to the modified electrode surface is easier [48].

In this study, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels and band gap energy (EGAP) of PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites were calculated by CV(Fig. 3e and 3(f)). HOMO, LUMO and band gap energy levels were determined by the following equations [58].
EHOMO =-e (Eox + ENHE + 4.75) (eV) (1)
ELUMO =-e (Ered + ENHE + 4.75)(eV) (2)
ENHE = EAg
/
AgCl: +0.197 (eV) (3)
EGAP = ELUMO − EHOMO (4)

Fig. 3(a) shows that only the Fe3+/Fe4+ reversible redox reaction was observed in GCE, while the GCE modified with 4AP N-GQDs and PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites showed a reduction peak at +0.034 and +0.476 V, and an oxidation peak at +0.448 +0.533 V, respectively. Accordingly, the E LUMO level is calculated as −4.98 and −5.42 eV, and the E HOMO level as −5.40 and −5.48 eV, respectively. Band gap energies for 4AP N-GQDs and PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanacomposites were 0.42 and 0.06 eV, respectively. Looking at the band gap energies of both materials, it can be said that the PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite has better conductivity.

The energy difference between the HOMO and LUMO, referred to as the HOMO-LUMO gap, is of great significance. It provides insights into a molecule's reactivity [59]. In a material, a smaller HOMO-LUMO gap usually means higher reactivity, which can be more advantageous for targeting biosensors as they will facilitate oxidation and/or reduction reactions.

The effects of the scan rates to the voltammograms of PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites were examined by CV technique too. As the scan rate increased in the range from 5 to 200 mVs−1, the location of the oxidation and reduction peaks shifted to higher and lower potentials, respectively (Fig. 3(f)), which was because there was a shorter time for the electrolyte ions to access the electrode’s surface and the effective active area was out of reach for the electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific capacitance values of the PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites modified-GCE at different scan rates
ce:section.ce:para:
The specific capacitances of PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites were calculated from CV curves at different scan rates ranging from 5 to 200 mVs−1 (Fig. 3(f)) [60]. These curves show that the electrode has a fast voltage inverting current response and low ion conduction resistance, and the specific capacitance values of PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites were calculated using the following formula [61]:
C= ∫IdV / vmΔV (5)

In this formula, ∫IdV is the area enclosed by the CV curve, ΔV (V) is the operating voltage window, v (mVs−1) is the scan rate and m (g) are the mass of PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites on the working electrode. These values were calculated as 0.777, 0.662, 0.584, 0.584, 0.580, 0.563, and 0.548F g−1 for scan rates of 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, and 200 mV s−1, respectively. The PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites showed the highest capacitive behavior with a specific capacitance value of 0.777F g−1 at a scan rate of 5 mVs−1 (Fig. 3(g)).

Electrochemical biosensors provide a fast and sensitive response, allow real-time monitoring, and can be miniaturized enough to be applied to living cells. However, biosensors must be able to provide a sensitive response in the nanomolar range for an unwell individual. Biosensors must also be selective enough to distinguish the measured analyte from interferences, such as biochemical ions and molecules oxidized within the same potential window as the measured analyte. Biosensors for the determination of DA must be selective enough to distinguish it from interferences such as ascorbic acid or uric acid, which oxidize within the same potential window as DA. The selectivity of electrochemical biosensor material to DA is important and challenging when working on samples of human serum or blood. In patient samples, there can be interferences from ascorbic acid or uric acid, and other neurotransmitters and bioactive molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CV analysis of DA on PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite modified GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 (a) shows oxidation peaks at 0.476 V and 0.504 V for the GCE, and the PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite modified GCE reduction peaks at 0.445 V for GCE, and at 0.472 V and 0.060 V for PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite modified GCE. respectively. Furthermore, PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite modified GCE showed electrocatalytic effect towards DA.

The increase in the square root of scan rates lead to a linear increase in peak current for anodic and cathodic reactions (Fig. 4(b)). An increasing scan rate resulted in a more positive anodic peak and a more negative cathodic peak, suggesting that this redox reaction is a quasi-reversible process.

The equations of the peak currents versus the square roots of the scan rates were found as Ipa = 0.97x + 3.0354 (R2 = 0.9947) and Ipc = −1.9584x + 4,438 (R2 = 0.993), respectively (Fig. 4(c)). Moreover the linear equations indicated that the electro-redox reactions of dopamine at the PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite electrode were the diffusion-controlled process and enhanced electrochemical activity.

The prepared PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite electrode was used as a DA sensor and found to exhibit a linear detection range of 250 pM-10 nM and a detection limit of 21 pM. In this study, better determination limits were obtained because the catechol groups of DA are easily adsorbed on the surface of PdNPs/4AP N-GDQDs and form hydrogen bonds with pyridinic-N due to the specific donor-bridge-acceptor structures of the sensor with a large, conjugated area. In addition, the large delocalized π-conjugated structure of the sensor forms complexes with DA more easily.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical mechanism of PdNPs/4AP N-GDQDs nanocomposite sensor to DA in an electrochemical cell
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidation reaction of N-GDQDs against DA is reportedly associated with the doped pyridinic-N structure in N-GDQDs and the catechol group in DA [62–66]. DA was oxidized to a quinone under an electric field via N-GDQDs/GCE and form a N-GDQDs and DA-quinone complex [48].

Scheme 3 shows that initially, PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites were coated on the GCE, forming a homogeneously dispersed porous structure. In the cell, the catechol hydrogens of DA form strong hydrogen bonds with pridinic-N on the PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs modified-GCE, further weakening the OH bond in the catechol. Therefore, DA generates.
–2H+ and −2e-, forming the catechol radical of DA and PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs2- forms. Finally, intramolecular rearrangement of dopamine radical leads to the formation of the quinone form.

Due to their structure with a large conjugated parous domain and a large number of donor–acceptor groups on the molecular surface, the DA phenol groups are easily adsorbed on the surface of PdNPs/4AP N-GDQD nanocomposites and interact with N-doped pyridinic-N to form strong hydrogen bonds. In addition, the large delocalized π-conjugated structures form a complex molecule with DA, causing DA to easily react electrochemically. This complex structure provides better selectivity for DA by excluding other interferents in the cell media.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DPV analysis of DA interaction with the PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidation peaks of 9 nM of DA were observed at 0.423 V as 4,47 µA for the GCE and at 0.488 V as 108 µA for the PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite electrode, respectively (Fig. 5 (a)). Fig. 5(a) reveals that the PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite modified GCE showed almost 24 times greater current than the control GCE.

DPV analysis of DA was performed with PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite modified GCE at different DA concentrations under optimized conditions. For this purpose, DA stock solution was prepared daily and diluted with ultrapure water for different DA concentrations. Good and stable oxidation peak curves of PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite modified-GCE in 0.1 M KCl containing 50 µM K3Fe(CN)6 and K4Fe(CN)6, and 0.1 mM DA obtained by DPV are given in Fig. 5(b). The DPV analysis was performed at the pulse amplitude 0.05 V, pulse time 0.04 s, voltage step 0.05 V, and voltage step time 0.1 s. The equation of the linear calibration graph was found as Ip/µA = 9x109 (μA/M) + 36.806, and the correlation coefficient was (r) = 0.998. The standard division (s) of peak currents was calculated as 21 pM by the formula of 3s / m (s is the standard division; m is the slope of the calibration curve).

For the ultrasensitive determination of DA by DPV, the developed PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite modified-GCE exhibited excellent sensitivity and improved performance compared with GCE control. The selectivity of the developed PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite material towards DA (linear detection range 250 pM-10 nM and detection limit 21 pM) was found to be more sensitive (low detection limits of 95 nM and 110 nM (S/N = 3) in the dynamic linear range of 0.25–250 μM) compared with its closest competitor in the literature [20]. In addition, previous studies for DA selectivity and the results of this study are given in Table 1 and analytical investigation of DA on PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite modified-GCE is given in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference DA detection by PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite modified-GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The main challenge with dopamine analysis is the presence of UA and AA, which have higher concentrations than DA and can interfere with its analysis [62]. In particular, possible interference between AA and UA should be considered when using electrochemical measurement of DA.

The effect of AA, CA, urea, CaCl2, Zn(CH3COO)2, and Na2SO4 on the oxidation peaks of DA was investigated with DPV (Fig. 6 (a)). For this, solutions of CaCl2, Zn(CH3COO)2, Na2SO4, AA, CA and urea at concentrations of 10 mM were added to the 0.1 M KCl electrolyte solution containing 10 mM DA, 50 µM K3Fe(CN)6 and K4Fe(CN)6. These interfering substances did not affect the detection performance of this sensor, indicating its high selectivity. It is possible that due to the specific donor-bridge-acceptor structures of the nanocomposite with a large conjugated and parous surface, it easily interacts with the catechol groups of DA, and thus a nanocomposite-DA complex is formed between the delocalized π-conjugated structures in the nanocomposite and DA. This complex provides better selectivity by excluding other interferents.
When Fig. 6(b) was analyzed, it is seen that the greatest interference effect comes from AA followed by Zn(CH3COO)2, while the interference of other compounds was little or negligible.

In this study, 4AP functionalized N-GQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal reaction of CA and 4AP. Then, PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites were prepared by the green method from the interaction of 4AP N-GQDs with Pd(II)nitrate. The synthesized 4AP N-GQDs and PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites were characterized by FT-IR, UV–Vis, TEM, EDX and XRD analyses. The prepared new PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite material was used for modification of GCE, which was subsequently used for the determination of DA by DPV. From these electrochemical determination studies by DPV, it was found that the developed ultra-sensor exhibited a linear determination range of 250 pM − 10 nM for DA. The developed PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposite material was found to be more sensitive to DA than its closest competitor in the literature. It was also found that the ultra-sensor was not affected by the interference of bioactive ions, Ca2+, Zn2+, Na+, Cl-, CH3COO–, SO4 2-, and biomolecules AA, CA and urea had no effect on oxidation peaks of DA.

The specific capacitances of PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites were calculated from CV curves at different scan rates ranging from 5 to 200 mVs−1, and PdNPs/4AP N-GQDs nanocomposites showed high capacitive behavior with a specific value of 0.777F g−1 at a scan rate of 5 mVs−1. It is evident that the newly developed nanocomposite material could be an excellent material for both ultra-biosensors in the determination of DA and energy storage with its high capacitance characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration on productive artificial intelligence and AI-enabled technologies the authoring process
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of the references in this study, the authors used Mendeley to ensure the reliability of the single and correct use of references. After using this tool/service, the authors took full responsibility and organized the references as required.
